item.title: In Memory of Hubble & the Amazing Images It Brought Us
item.description:
The James Webb Space Telescope has dropped even more incredible shots of the universe today, and User Sub is filling with them as it should. But let's take a moment to appreciate some of the incredible insights into the universe its big brother Hubble brought us over the last decades.
"This image by the Hubble Space Telescope is the most comprehensive view yet of the universe's evolution as seen by a space telescope. The coloful image, released June 3, 2014, contains 10,000 galaxies, with the different colors denoting different wavelengths. (Image credit: NASA, ESA, H. Teplitz and M. Rafelski (IPAC/Caltech), A. Koekemoer (STScI), R. Windhorst (Arizona State University), and Z. Levay (STScI))" https://www.space.com/26118-colorful-hubble-telescope-photo-universe-evolution.html Until the reshoot of it was released yesterday by The James Webb Space Telescope, the Hubble Deep Field images were considered the most important ever captured by humanity, and with good reason. For the first time they showed us with clarity the history of how the universe was not long after The Big Bang. It's unlikely however there'll be a telescope with enough resolution to let us see before The Big Bang.
"The Hubble mosaic unveils a collection of carved knots of gas and dust in a small portion of the Monkey Head Nebula (also known as NGC 2174 and Sharpless Sh2-252). The nebula is a star-forming region that hosts dusky dust clouds silhouetted against glowing gas." "Massive, newly formed stars near the center of the nebula (and toward the right in this image) are blasting away at dust within the nebula. Ultraviolet light from these bright stars helps carve the dust into giant pillars. The nebula is mostly composed of hydrogen gas, which becomes ionized by the ultraviolet radiation." "As the interstellar dust particles are warmed from the radiation from the stars in the center of the nebula, they heat up and begin to glow at infrared wavelengths." "The image demonstrates Hubble's powerful infrared vision and offers a tantalizing hint of what scientists can expect from the upcoming James Webb Space Telescope." Wow were they right to be excited. And damn have the JWST's results been amazing today.
"Images of the “Pillars of Creation” are among the most recognizable of the thousands the Hubble Space Telescope has created. The butte-like features were captured in the Eagle Nebula, also known as M16." Hehe, butte-like.
"The shot above is of a star-forming region in the Carina Nebula. The enormous pillar of gas and dust is 3 light-years tall. The seam in the middle is the result of new stars forming and emitting powerful gas jets that are ripping the pillar apart." It's not just enormous, it's at least a small boulder the size of a large boulder enormous.
"The Butterfly Nebula shows what happens to a star at the end of its life, when it loses all of its gas and dust to its surroundings. Not only is this a reminder to the eventual fate of our own Sun and Solar System, but Hubble's unique ability to witness this event in a star's long life cycle sheds light on how stars evolve." I can't even imagine how huge the very hungry caterpillar which came before it must have been.
"Massive stars off the top of this image emit ultraviolet radiation that erodes the edges of the Cone Nebula (NGC 2264). This process liberates gas, and the ultraviolet light then excites this hydrogen-rich material, causing it to glow with a characteristic red color." The nebula which brought us The Coneheads, that important bong bit, & VLC is staggering in its beauty.
"V838 Monocerotis: In January 2002, the star seen at center flared to become one of our galaxy’s most luminous. This view from two years later captures surrounding dust shells lit up by the eruption." I can't be the only person who sees a circular galactic embryo with a tail wrapped around a central cluster of stars waiting to be born.
"The Spirograph Nebula" "The progenitor star of the Spirograph Nebula (IC 418) cast off its outer layers multiple times, filling the planetary’s interior with lots of gas. A white dwarf stands out at the heart of the nebula; such stars energize the nebula’s gas and cause it to glow." The amount of ink you'd need in your pen to make a spirograph that huge is something to consider. So shoutout to The Hubble Space Telescope for the perspective changing images it brought to us over its life which are worth celebrating, and to the stupendously superior shots the James Webb Space Telescope may bring us for decades into the future.
item.dc:creator: graehall

item.title: TLDR: Fuck A Middleman
item.description: I freelance for a private tutoring school and couldn't figure out why so many of my regular students weren't returning. Turns out the school raised all of their class fees (without giving me any notice or sharing the profits) and one of the parents complained directly to me about it. I'm not under contract because they've never offered me one so I told the parents they could pay me what they were paying the school and we would carry on as though nothing had changed. Rinse and repeat with a couple of other students and I'll probably be out of a job with the school but I'll get to keep working with all of the same kids I've gotten to know and net myself a raise in the process.
item.dc:creator: ImmortalChild

item.title: James Webb Space Telescope.
item.description:
The incredible thing about the James Webb Telescope image is not that it is "just a higher resolution image", it is that literally, for the first time in the entire history of mankind, we can see the first galaxies that formed in the universe. Credit: cakocorvalan.
NASA has revealed the first scientific image from the James Webb telescope. The deepest and sharpest image of the early universe ever taken.
item.dc:creator: dolphinyaso

item.title: The happiest year of her entire life
item.description:
Hi everyone, I'm MrPuckett
Many of you have seen me post about Nanna, my 16 year old blind hospice dog who was surrendered to Nashville Humane
Today is a very special day in the Nanna timeline, a day that I never once thought was possible -
Today is the one year anniversary of the day we adopted Nanna Pants!!!! She's still alive and thriving!
We only thought she was going to live for a few months, she was in such rough shape when she was surrendered My over/under was 3 months - if she made it past 3 months then we'd celebrate, and if she didn't then at least we gave her as much love as possible
Now it's 12 months later and Nanna is the healthiest version of herself ＼(°o°)／ And though I don't know much about her life before me, I think it's pretty clear by the shape she was in that this is the happiest year of her entire life.
She could barely even stand up when she first came home and now she's crushing 5ks like it's no big deal At 16 years old! It's absolutely insane!!!
She was also terrified of my dogs when she first came home So scared to be in a new place, blind, as random dogs run around her
Safe to say she's overcome all of those fears and has made a special bond with both MrsPuckett and PJ
Pizza Angels @testoubrei @NoRoutesFound and @MurphyPandorasLawBox came down to Nashville this summer and we all went on vacation together with the dogs. It was amazing to see Nanna so nervous that first night and by day two she was having the time of her life
Everything they do, they do it together as a family
I wanted ALL the tourist photos on vacation
I'm proud that we gave her a home and the tools she needed to get healthy again, but there's no doubt that she's provided us with way more than that
She's taught us that it's never too late to start over, it's never too late to be the happiest and healthiest version of yourself We never stop growing.
And most importantly - she's taught us that with enough love, just about anything is possible. Happy one year anniversary Nanna Pants, we love you more than words could ever say. ... We also added a new Puckett to the pack, more on him later this week https://i.imgur.com/4wsda7I.jpg ... We're coming up on the 3 year anniversary of TheFreePizzaDude! Big plans in just a couple of weeks. TheFreePizzaDude 3 year anniversary! https://imgur.com/a/Gn4Dgg4 ... Have a beautiful day everyone
item.dc:creator: MrPuckett

item.title: Lord, we’re done down here!!
item.description: It’s 106° today. No end in sight for triple digits. I’m sure rolling blackouts are coming soon. Can’t handle the heat, can’t handle the cold! Most viral edit: heck yeah!! We just got solar panels but haven’t had them a full billing cycle yet so that will be interesting! And I’ll tell you this, my A/C is NOT set on 95 or 78 or any other foolishness! I’m lucky enough to have a pool so I’m in it when I’m outside at all.
item.dc:creator: ScubaGirlTX
